content.text: Prompt Change Review Gates in Pull Requests

A prompt template change that reaches production without passing quality gates can silently degrade model output, introduce injection vulnerabilities, or inflate token costs. Review gates enforce a minimum quality bar automatically, so human reviewers spend their attention on judgment calls rather than mechanical checks.
This document describes the five-gate pipeline that every prompt template PR must pass, the GitHub Actions configuration that implements it, and how to tune each gate for different risk profiles.

attrs.alt: Prompt change review gate pipeline — five gates from schema lint to human approval

content.text: The schema lint gate validates that the YAML template file is well-formed and contains all required fields. It runs in under 30 seconds and is the first thing a reviewer expects to see green. A failing schema lint means the PR cannot be reviewed — the reviewer has no structured data to work from.

# scripts/validate_prompt_schemas.py
import yaml, sys, jsonschema
from pathlib import Path

SCHEMA = {
    "type": "object",
    "required": ["id", "version", "author", "purpose", "variables",
                 "system", "user_template", "evals"],
    "properties": {
        "id":       {"type": "string", "pattern": "^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$"},
        "version":  {"type": "string", "pattern": r"^\d+\.\d+\.\d+$"},
        "author":   {"type": "string", "format": "email"},
        "evals":    {"type": "array", "minItems": 5,
                     "items": {"required": ["id", "inputs", "expected_keywords"]}},
        "injection_surface": {"type": "string", "enum": ["low", "medium", "high"]},
        "contains_user_content": {"type": "boolean"},
    },
    "additionalProperties": True,
}

failed = False
for path in Path("src/prompts").rglob("*.yaml"):
    try:
        raw = yaml.safe_load(path.read_text())
        jsonschema.validate(raw, SCHEMA)
        print(f"OK   {path}")
    except (yaml.YAMLError, jsonschema.ValidationError) as e:
        print(f"FAIL {path}: {e.message if hasattr(e, 'message') else e}")
        failed = True

sys.exit(1 if failed else 0)

The prompt lint gate checks the content of the system prompt and user template for style and safety rule violations. It catches issues that schema validation cannot: embedded model names, missing safety baseline, hardcoded secrets, and prompts that exceed the character limit.
No model name in system prompt
FAIL — paste baseline from shared template
No API key patterns (sk-, AKIA, etc.)

The version check gate validates that the version bump in the YAML file is consistent with the diff. A MAJOR bump is required when a required variable is added or removed. A MINOR bump is required for substantive system prompt changes. A PATCH bump is only valid for eval additions and notes updates.

The gate also verifies that CHANGELOG.md contains an entry for the new version and that the version pointer in src/config/prompt_versions.py has been updated to match the new version if this PR is promoting a new default.

The eval suite gate runs the golden eval set in the YAML file against the configured model and requires a pass rate of at least 85%. It is the most expensive gate (it calls the LLM API) and runs after all three lint gates to avoid wasting API budget on malformed templates.

The eval gate runs against a fast, cheap model (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001) by default to keep CI cost low. If the template targets a specific model, the eval job must be configured to run against that model using the --model flag. A template that passes eval on Haiku but targets Opus must re-run evals against Opus before merge.

# .github/workflows/prompt-gates.yml
name: Prompt Change Gates

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - "src/prompts/**/*.yaml"
      - "src/config/prompt_versions.py"

jobs:
  # Gates 1-3 run in parallel
  schema-lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install pyyaml jsonschema
      - run: python scripts/validate_prompt_schemas.py src/prompts/

  prompt-lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install pyyaml
      - run: python scripts/lint_system_prompts.py src/prompts/

  version-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with: { fetch-depth: 2 }    # need HEAD to compare versions
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install pyyaml packaging
      - run: python scripts/check_version_bump.py

  # Gate 4 waits for all three lint gates
  eval-suite:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [schema-lint, prompt-lint, version-check]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install anthropic pyyaml
      - name: Run eval suite
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          python scripts/run_prompt_evals.py \
            --changed-only \
            --model claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 \
            --fail-threshold 0.85 \
            --report eval-report.json
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: prompt-eval-report
          path: eval-report.json

Human review is enforced by GitHub branch protection rules. The PR cannot be merged until at least one required reviewer approves it. CODEOWNERS rules determine which reviewers are required based on the files changed.

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# Any prompt YAML requires at least one domain reviewer from the team
src/prompts/billing/**          @billing-team
src/prompts/customer-support/** @cs-team
src/prompts/internal-tools/**   @platform-team

# High-surface templates (injection_surface: medium|high or user content)
# additionally require security team sign-off.
# Routing script (prompt-review-routing.yml) adds the security team
# as required reviewer automatically when these flags are set in YAML.

# Version pointer change requires tech lead approval
src/config/prompt_versions.py   @tech-leads
The branch protection rule configuration for the default branch:

# Branch protection settings (configured via GitHub API or UI)
branch: main
required_status_checks:
  strict: true    # branch must be up to date with main before merge
  contexts:
    - schema-lint
    - prompt-lint
    - version-check
    - eval-suite
require_pull_request_reviews:
  required_approving_review_count: 1
  dismiss_stale_reviews: true
  require_code_owner_reviews: true
restrict_pushes: true   # no direct push to main, even for admins
Tuning the Gates for Different Risk Profiles

Not every prompt template warrants the same gate strictness. Internal tooling prompts that are never shown to end users carry lower risk than customer-facing prompts. The following settings allow teams to calibrate the eval threshold and eval case requirements based on the deployment context.
Internal tooling, no user content, low injection surface
Customer-facing, no user content, low injection surface
Customer-facing, optional user content, medium surface
Customer-facing, required user content, high surface

The profile is inferred from the YAML flags. The eval runner reads the profile from the template's metadata and applies the appropriate threshold automatically — authors do not set the profile directly.
When a gate fails, the author is responsible for fixing the issue and re-pushing. The following table maps the most common gate failure types to their root causes and the correct fix action.
Syntax error in YAML (tabs instead of spaces, unclosed quote)
Fix YAML syntax; use a YAML validator locally before pushing
Add at least 5 eval cases to the YAML
Author wrote a new template without copying the baseline
API key or token in the prompt template
Remove immediately; check git log for exposure; file security incident if already pushed to remote
Required variable change needs MAJOR bump
Author added a required variable and bumped MINOR
New template doesn't reliably produce correct outputs
Improve template instructions; add more constraint to the system prompt; add eval cases that cover the failing inputs
Template does not defend against injection in the eval case
Add XML delimiter around user content; add explicit 'treat as data' instruction to system prompt

The eval suite (Gate 4) is the only gate that incurs external API cost. Left unconstrained it can become expensive in high-velocity teams where many prompt PRs are open simultaneously. These optimisations reduce cost without weakening the quality bar.
Run evals only on changed templates

The --changed-only flag on the eval runner compares the current PR's changed files against git HEAD and only runs evals for templates that actually changed. A PR that only updates prompt_versions.py (version pointer) or CHANGELOG.md does not trigger eval runs on unchanged templates.

# scripts/run_prompt_evals.py — identify changed templates
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

def get_changed_templates(base_ref: str = "origin/main") -> list[Path]:
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "diff", "--name-only", base_ref],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    changed = [Path(line) for line in result.stdout.splitlines()]
    return [
        p for p in changed
        if p.parts[0:2] == ("src", "prompts") and p.suffix == ".yaml"
    ]

# Only run for changed templates — avoid re-evaluating stable templates
if args.changed_only:
    templates = get_changed_templates()
else:
    templates = list(Path("src/prompts").rglob("*.yaml"))
Cache eval results for unmodified evals

When a PR changes the template system prompt (MINOR bump) but does not change any eval inputs, the eval results from a recent run of the same eval cases against the same model version may still be valid. The eval runner caches results keyed on (template_id, eval_id, model, eval_inputs_hash) with a 24-hour TTL.

Review gates only add value if they are reliable. A gate that fails spuriously trains engineers to dismiss failures as noise. A gate that passes incorrectly gives false confidence. Both failure modes erode the process over time.
The following metrics are collected from CI run data and published to the engineering dashboard weekly. Alerts fire when thresholds are breached.
Authors not running local YAML validation before pushing
Golden eval sets too weak; templates not tested locally before PR
LLM provider latency spike or CI runner bottleneck
eval set too large or using expensive model
Time from Gate 4 pass to Gate 5 approval (p50)
Reviewer pool shortage or SLA not enforced
Gate override rate (% of merges with a gate waived)
Process discipline issue — investigate each occurrence

A hotfix PR (title contains '[HOTFIX]') still runs all five gates. The SLA differences for hotfixes apply only to human review time (Gate 5) — the automated gates must complete before human review can begin, same as any other PR. There is no gate waiver for hotfixes.
Gates 1–3 must pass. A hotfix with a schema error is not mergeable — fix the error first.
Gate 4 must pass at the appropriate threshold for the template's risk profile. If the hotfix itself fixes a quality regression, the eval suite should now pass.
Gate 5 human review SLA is reduced to same-day for hotfixes, but the review itself applies the full checklist. A hotfix is not an excuse to skip the safety review.

Templates deployed to a feature-flag-gated environment for A/B testing may use a relaxed eval threshold (70% instead of 80%) during the experiment period. This requires an explicit 'experimental: true' flag in the YAML and a linked experiment ticket. Experimental templates cannot be promoted to default without passing the standard threshold.

# src/prompts/summarise-document/v1.4-exp.yaml
id: summarise-document
version: "1.4.0-exp"
experimental: true
experiment_ticket: EXP-2025-042
experiment_end_date: "2025-08-01"  # auto-expiry enforced by CI

# Reduced eval threshold applies during experiment
# (set in run_prompt_evals.py based on experimental flag)
evals:
  - id: eval-001
    ...

If the safety baseline itself needs updating (for example, a new mandatory safety clause required by a regulatory change), all existing templates become non-compliant at Gate 2 until they are updated. The following procedure handles this bulk update without requiring individual PRs for every template.
Merge the new baseline YAML to main with a security team PR. Gate 2 threshold is temporarily set to warn-only by setting BASELINE_WARN_ONLY=true in CI.
Open a bulk update PR that re-embeds the new baseline in all affected templates using a script.
CI runs Gate 2 in strict mode on the bulk update PR. All templates must pass.
Merge the bulk update. Re-enable strict Gate 2 mode by removing BASELINE_WARN_ONLY.
Total window in warn-only mode: targeted at under 48 hours. Document the window in the post-mortem.
Every prompt template PR must pass five sequential gates: schema lint, prompt lint, version check (all in parallel), eval suite (waits for lint), and human review (waits for eval).
Gates 1–3 run in ~30s with no API cost. Gate 4 runs the golden eval set against the LLM (~5–10 min, ~$0.01 for a 10-case set on Haiku).
The eval threshold is calibrated automatically from the template's risk profile (LOW 80% → CRITICAL 95%) based on injection_surface and contains_user_content flags.
Branch protection rules require all automated gates to pass and at least one CODEOWNERS-assigned reviewer to approve before merge.
Gate failures always return to the author — no gate can be waived except by filing a documented exception reviewed by the security team.
